summary: McKenzie describes a weekend spent on minor but important fixes to his Bingo Card Creator product and website. He's expanding the Wizard functionality to support math classes beyond reading. He discovered his website links weren't underlined, making them invisible as clickable elements, and plans to fix this after seeing clickstream data showing users repeatedly clicking tabs trying to navigate.
key_points: Inconsistency in product UI naming (Dolch Word Lists vs Dolch Sight Words) needed cleanup, though website inconsistency is deliberately kept for SEO synonym capture
Website links lacked underlines, making them unrecognizable as links — a basic usability failure he missed for weeks
Clickstream data revealed users double-clicking tabs, suggesting navigation confusion rather than user error
Previously doubled downloads in a day just by adding buttons, reinforcing that visual affordances matter enormously
money_quotes: While inconsistency is fine for the website (got to catch search engine synonyms) I wanted the UI to look a wee bit more professional.
Here's the other thing I just figured out: my links do not look like links, because they are not underlined.
I can't believe that I've missed that for the entire time I've been working on my website.
Many many people trying to get to the free trial, for example, click the Free Trial tab and then click it again.
I doubled my downloads in a day by putting up those buttons — making the links scream "I'm a link!" can't possibly hurt.
key_insight: Basic visual affordances like underlined links and clear buttons have an outsized impact on conversion — small usability fixes that seem trivial can dramatically change user behavior.
